coredata.dc:title: Repairing surface defects of carbon fibers: Electrostatic spraying nano-carbons on stabilized PAN-fibers and subsequent thermal annealing
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Tianshuai
Shao, Ruiqi
Siddique, Amna
Wang, Wei
Li, Tianyu
Jiang, Wanwei
Pei, Xiaoyuan
Xu, Zhiwei
coredata.dc:description: The introducing of nanomaterials on the carbon fibers (CFs) surface is an effective method for repairing surface defects. Due to the chemical inertness of CFs surface, the bonding force between reinforcing material and CFs is weak. In this paper, nano-carbons (graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots) were assembled to defects on surface of stabilized fibers (SFs) through electrostatic spraying assisted by airflow and then thermally annealed to enhance bonding force between nano-carbons and fibers. The distribution of nano-carbons on fibers surface was explored and it was found that the nano-carbons were directionally covered on or filled in defects on fibers surface to reduce specific surface area and pore volume. The chemical structures on fiber surface were discussed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and atomistic ReaxFF molecular dynamics simulation, and it suggested that covalent crosslinking occurred between CFs surface and nano-carbons. And further, the nano-scratch on fibers surface revealed a significant increase in the bonding force between nano-carbons and fiber after thermal annealing. The maximum tensile strength and Young´s modulus of CFs with nano-carbons increased 24.36% and 38.63%, respectively. Subsequently, the finite element simulation ABAQUS was adopted to demonstrate that the addition of nano-carbons alleviated stress concentration at defects on CFs surface. At the same time, the smaller stress and stress diffusion were found in CFs reinforced by graphene oxide than that with graphene quantum dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface defects
Carbon fibers
Nano-carbons
Covalent crosslinking

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spraying of nano-carbons on stabilized fibers
Simulation of electrostatic spraying and stress distribution by physical fields
Simulations of thermal annealing by atomistic ReaxFF MD
Assembly of nano-carbons on surface defects of SFs
Surface morphology of SFs and CFs with nano-carbons
Surface chemical changes on fibers with nano-carbons induced by thermal annealing
Changes in bonding force between nano-carbons and fiber surface
Enhancement mechanisms of nano-carbons in mechanical properties

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12205218 and 12102300) and Nantong Science and Technology Project (JC12022089). We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for X-ray Diffractometer, Thermal Field-Emission Scanning Electron Microscope, Transmission Electron Microscope and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of nano-scratch; Loading process of force and displament: (b) SFs-GO, (c) CFs-GO.
(a) Illustration of electrostatic spraying; Finite element simulation of electric field (b) and airflow field (c).

TEM of GQDs (a) and GO (b); SEM images: (c) pristine SFs, (d) SFs-GO, (e) SFs-GQDs, (f) pristine CFs, (g) CFs-GO, (h) CFs-GQDs, (i) partial magnification of (h); (j) Pore diameter of fibers; (k) Specific surface area and pore volume of CFs.

XPS spectra of C1s peaks: (a) pristine SFs, (b) SFs-GO, (c) SFs-GQDs, (d) pristine CFs, (e) CFs-GO, (f) CFs-GQDs; (g) XRD; (h) Raman spectra; (i) Schematic illustration for removal of non-carbon atoms and formation of covalent bonds.

Atomistic ReaxFF simulations of thermal annealing: (a), (d), (g) and (i) for snapshots of GO, GQDs, SFs-GO and SFs-GQDs respectively; Crosslinking structure: (b) and (c) for GO, (e) and (f) for GQDs, (h) for SFs-GO, (j) and (k) for SF-GQDs.

Morphology of GO by optical microscope and SEM: (a) and (b) on SFs, (g) and (h) on CFs; Surface morphology before and after scratching: (c) and (d) for SFs-GO, (i) and (j) for CFs-GO; Lateral displacement and force during scratching: (e) and (f) for SFs-GO, (k) and (l) for CFs-GO.
(a) Tensile strength of fibers; (b) Young´s modulus of fibers; (c)Schematic diagram of covalent bonds between fibers and nano-carbons.

Models for finite element simulation: (a) pristine CFs, (d) CFs-GQDs and (g) CFs-GO; Stress distribution for (b) pristine CFs, (e) CFs-GQDs and (h) CFs-GO; (c), (f) and (i) for partial magnification of stress distribution marked by a rectangle.
Schematic diagram of crack propagation: (a) pristine CFs, (b) CFs-GQDs, (c) CFs-GO.

head.ce:title: Repairing surface defects of carbon fibers: Electrostatic spraying nano-carbons on stabilized PAN-fibers and subsequent thermal annealing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dChangsheng (Langfang) Technology Co., Ltd., Langfang High Tech Industrial Development Zone, Anci District, Langfang, Hebei Province 065002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The introducing of nanomaterials on the carbon fibers (CFs) surface is an effective method for repairing surface defects. Due to the chemical inertness of CFs surface, the bonding force between reinforcing material and CFs is weak. In this paper, nano-carbons (graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots) were assembled to defects on surface of stabilized fibers (SFs) through electrostatic spraying assisted by airflow and then thermally annealed to enhance bonding force between nano-carbons and fibers. The distribution of nano-carbons on fibers surface was explored and it was found that the nano-carbons were directionally covered on or filled in defects on fibers surface to reduce specific surface area and pore volume. The chemical structures on fiber surface were discussed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and atomistic ReaxFF molecular dynamics simulation, and it suggested that covalent crosslinking occurred between CFs surface and nano-carbons. And further, the nano-scratch on fibers surface revealed a significant increase in the bonding force between nano-carbons and fiber after thermal annealing. The maximum tensile strength and Young´s modulus of CFs with nano-carbons increased 24.36% and 38.63%, respectively. Subsequently, the finite element simulation ABAQUS was adopted to demonstrate that the addition of nano-carbons alleviated stress concentration at defects on CFs surface. At the same time, the smaller stress and stress diffusion were found in CFs reinforced by graphene oxide than that with graphene quantum dots.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to resistance of high and low temperature, corrosion resistance, high specific strength and high specific modulus, carbon fibers (CFs) had been widely used in many fields, such as aerospace, petrochemical, sports, architecture, medical treatment, and transportation [1-3]. However, the tensile strength of CFs is less than 5% of the theoretical value (180 GPa), and the Young's modulus can only reach 68% of the theoretical value (1020 GPa), indicating that there is still great potential for improving the mechanical properties of CFs [4-6]. It was known that defects in CFs severely restricted their mechanical properties [7,8]. CFs was a kind of brittle materials and its tensile strength had a strong dependence on defects [9]. During the stretching process of CFs, it is easy to cause the stress concentration at the defects, leading to the fracture of CFs. It had been proved that the tensile strength was inversely proportional to the size of the defect, and minimizing the size and number of defects led to higher tensile strengths [10]. There was a number of defects in CFs, divided into two kinds: surface defects and internal defects. The surface defects included surface cracks, twisting and bending in microfibers and grooves. Internal defects included microvoids, heteroatoms, disoriented structures and etc. It should be pointed out that the surface defects of CFs account for majority of the total number of defects. And according to the Griffith formula, it can be concluded that the surface defects of CFs made a greater contribution in the decline of tensile strength than internal defects [11]. It was known that surface defects originate from fiber shrinkage and gas evolution during the heat treatment processes, as well as from post surface treatment before applying sizing agents to enhance interfacial strength [12]. The surface flaws can easily initiate fractures which propagate inside by stress concentration, when tensile force is applied [13,14]. Therefore, repairing the surface defects of CFs to improve mechanical properties has a great prospect.

Related studies had been conducted to enhance the mechanical properties of CFs by reducing surface defects through etching, coating, heteroatom modifications, and etc. [9,15-18]. Compared to other methods, coating technology has a series of advantages, such as: simple process, abundant raw materials, and significant improvement in mechanical properties. Therefore, in order to improve the tensile strength, coatings are popular in reducing defects on the surface of CFs. CFs had been coated with polymers, nano-carbons and ceramic materials by using impregnation, electrostatic spraying, electrophoretic deposition, chemical vapor deposition and etc. [19-23], and the tensile properties of CFs were enhanced by early failure of coatings and healing of defects. Among coating materials, nano-carbons are widely used due to their excellent structure and performance [17,24-26]. Guo et al. [24] prepared a dense carbon nanotube coating on the surface of CFs by ultrasonically assisted electrophoretic deposition. Through modifying the defects, the tensile strength of the fibers had been significantly improved. Wang et al. [25] found that, under ultrasonic treatment, graphene oxide (GO) was deposited on CFs using electrophoretic deposition method and the tensile strength of CFs increased by 14.1%. In order to further improve mechanical properties, the thermal annealing was carried out at 150 °C to form covalent bonds between GO and CFs, which made tensile strength further increase. Compared to other nano-carbons, due to the rich of oxygen-containing functional groups in GO, strong crosslink bonds between GO and CFs were easier formed, which contributed more to the improvement of tensile strength than simple intermolecular force. In previous reports, nano-carbons were covered on the surface of CFs and then subjected to additional heat treatment. However, the nano-carbons uniformly covered the entire surface of CFs instead of precisely repairing surface defects, and due to the surface inertness of CFs and low temperature for annealing on CFs surface with nano-carbons, few covalent bonds were formed [25,27].

It had been reported that graphene was added into PAN fibers during spinning, and then the PAN fibers with graphene were carbonized to form CFs [28]. The edges of graphene could form covalent bonds with the polymer matrix to form larger graphite structures in CFs, which made a 225% increase in tensile strength and a 184% increase in Young's modulus of CFs. It provided a new idea for post-treatment of coating. Inspired by this, introducing GO into the surface defects of stabilized PAN fibers (SFs) is expected to be an effective means for enhancing the mechanical properties of CFs.

In recent years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were widely used in many fields such as anticancer therapy, biomedical imaging, and etc. [29,30]. In our previous report [31], the GQDs with ultrafine particle size was produced through solvothermal method, and it has a structure similar to that of GO. Compared with GO, the particle size of GQDs was smaller, which made it easier enter the surface defects and reinforce CFs.

Due to the insulation and temperature sensitivity of the SFs, how to introduce nano-carbons in defects on SFs surface remained to be explored. In recent years, electrostatic spraying technology has attracted much attention due to its unique advantages (e.g. controllability, uniform distribution, one-step) in the preparation of surface modifications, environmental remediation, medical engineering and micro-nano thin membrane [32-35]. In the process of electrostatic spraying, the solution in the needle would be atomized to form small droplets with the same charge to move directionally onto the receiving plate. Liu et al. [35] used electrostatic spraying to evenly cover graphene oxide quantum dots and ethylenediamine on the surface of basement membrane. Wang et al. [36] prepared MWCNTs/WPU superhydrophobic conductive coatings with micro-rough surfaces by electrostatic spraying. Therefore, fixing nano-carbons on the surface of SFs by electrostatic spraying method is a feasible method.

In this study, nano-carbons (GO and GQDs) were covered on the defects of SFs surface innovatively through electrostatic spraying, and then the SFs with nano-carbons was thermally annealed to form CFs with strong bonding nano-carbons in one step (combined carbonization and post heat treatment). Compared with previous reports, the nano-carbons directionally repaired surface defects of CFs and strong bonding (with the help of high temperature during thermal annealing for preparing CFs) was formed between CFs surface and nano-carbons. The tensile strength and Young's modulus of CFs with nano-carbons was measured. The morphology of nano-carbons on fiber surface and changes in microstructure after thermal annealing were observed, and the covalent bonds between nano-carbons and CFs surface were suggested to be formed. The nano-scratch was used to detect the formation of strong bonding between CFs and nano-carbons after thermal annealing. In order to further study the formation of covalent bonds (between nano-carbons and CFs surface) and the reinforcement effect of nano-carbons with different sizes on the mechanics of CFs, the atomistic ReaxFF molecular dynamics (MD) simulation and ABAQUS were adopted.

PAN with 12,000 filaments per tow was purchased from Jilin Tangu Carbon Fiber Co., Ltd. The graphite (500 mesh) adopted in the study was purchased from Qingdao Ruisheng Graphite Co., Ltd. DMF was obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The γ-irradiation was carried out in Beijing Hongyi Sifang Radiation Technology Co., Ltd. The concentration of sulfuric acid, phosphoric acid, and hydrochloric acid were 95%−98%, more than 85%, and 36%−38%, respectively, which were purchased from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technologies Co., Ltd.

First, thermal stabilization of the PAN fibers was conducted in an oven with an automatic heating program. During the stabilization process, the fibers were fixed on the top of the oven with a stretching stress of 4 MPa. The temperature was programmed as the following successively:210–235–242–250–265 °C, and the total time was 136 min.

The graphite powder was irradiated by 60Co γ-rays in air, and the irradiation dose was 250 kGy with a rate of 2.7 kGy/h. Then the γ-irradiated graphite was used to prepare GO and GQDs via the modified Hummers method and solvothermal method shown in our previous work [31]. It should be pointed out that GQDs prepared with the assistance of γ-irradiation possessed ultrafine particle size and centralized distribution, which was beneficial for the entry of GQDs in defects on fibers surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spraying of nano-carbons on stabilized fibers
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs obtained from the solvothermal method were in a solution with DMFs, and the concentration of GQDs was about 4 mg/ml for spraying. A certain amount of GO was taken out and added into DMF to get a solution with a concentration of 4 mg/ml and then the solution was placed in an ultrasonic equipment for 30 min to obtain a uniform suspension for spraying. Firstly, the SFs were placed on a self-made frame, and then the frame was fixed on a metal receiving plate earthed. The voltage applied on the nozzle was 25 kV, and the distance between the fibers and the nozzle was 5 cm. The extrusion speed of the spraying solution was 2 ml/h with a total spraying time of about 10 h. During the spraying process, the frame with fibers needed to be manually flipped over to make the nano-carbons cover the whole fiber. The SFs without nano-carbons were called pristine SFs, and the SFs with GO and GQDs were called SFs-GO and SFs-GQDs, respectively.

Finally, the SFs were heated to fabricate CFs. The thermal annealing was carried out in a tube furnace filled with nitrogen gas and the heating program was set as: 20 °C-600 °C with a heating rate of 20 °C/min, 600 °C to 1350 °C with a heating rate of 25 °C/min and then a natural cooling. The CFs without nano-carbons were called pristine CFs and the CFs with GO and GQDs were called CFs-GO and CFs-GQDs, respectively.

The mechanical properties of CFs and SFs were obtained by a universal testing machine with a weighing transducer (100 N). The fiber was tested by bonding a filament on a gauge paper case with a vacancy of 20 mm, and then the fiber was broken by cutting off both sides of the paper and employing a stretching with a speed of 5 mm/min. More than 20 filaments for each specimen were measured. The morphology and microstructure of fibers were observed by a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM 500). The graphitization degree of CFs was investigated by Raman spectroscopy on a micro-Raman spectrometer equipped with an Ar+ laser with 532 nm, and the size of the laser spot was about 2 µm. The lattice information was calculated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Ka radiation and operated at 40 kV and 150 mA. The distribution of the functional group on the fiber surface was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI-5700). Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were separately collected by an Autosorb-iQ to analyze the pore structure of CFs. The Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model was used to determine the specific surface area and distribution of pore size.

To explore the bonding force between nano-carbons and fibers, the nano-scratch was performed by Bruker TI 980 TriboIndenter system. The Berkovich indenter with a circular needle tip was adopted. Scanning probe microscopy was applied to investigate the morphology and select the scratch position of specimen surface. The schematic diagram of nano-scratch and loading process was shown in Fig. 1 . It should be pointed out that the GQDs was too small and filled into the defects, which limited the use of nano-scratch. On the contrary, GO had a large size and was covered on fiber surface, which facilitated the selection of the test location and the conduct of scratch. At the same time, GO had similar structures with GQDs except for different sizes. After thermal annealing, the change in the bonding force of GO could represent the evolution in bonding force of GQDs. For SFs-GO, due to the weak bonding between the SFs surface and GO, the scratch was carried out with a constant penetration depth of 20 nm and a lateral displacement of 10 μm. As to CFs-GO, due to the form of strong bonding between the CFs surface and GO, a ramp load with a maximum force of 1000 μN was used for scratch in a lateral displacement of 1.4 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation of electrostatic spraying and stress distribution by physical fields
ce:section.ce:para: The distribution of electric fields and the flow of air during spraying were studied by finite element method using a COMSOL Multiphysics package with AC/DC and Fluid flow modules, respectively. It should be pointed out that two simulations were conducted independently. The simulation cell geometry in COMSOL was shown in Fig. 2 (b, c). In the simulation of electric potential distribution, the “static electricity (es)” in AC/DC module was used with a receiving plate connected to earth ground and a voltage of 25 kV at the needle. In the simulation of flow of air, the “turbulence, k-ε (spf)” in the Fluid flow module including Renault average Navier-Stokes equations (RANS) was used with inlet velocity 4 m/s (calculated from air pressure 0.011 MPa in Fig. 2(a)).

In order to investigate the influence of different material shapes on the optimization of surface defects in CFs, ABAQUS was used to conduct finite element simulation on the surface-treated CFs and study the changes of Von-Mises stress distribution. The geometric structures of CFs, GQDs, and GO were discretization by two-dimensional solid elements CPS4R and CPS3. The material behavior of CFs follows an anisotropic elastic constitutive model, as shown in Table S1. The material behavior of GQDs and GO follows a linear isotropic elastic constitutive model, with an elastic modulus of 200,000 and a Poisson's ratio of 0.3. The interaction between CFs and the two materials was achieved through binding. An inward pressure of 5 MPa was applied on the surface of the model and the variation of Von-Mises stress in the model was observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulations of thermal annealing by atomistic ReaxFF MD
ce:section.ce:para: Here, ReaxFF MD simulations with C/H/O/N force field [37-39] were used to investigate two problems at the atomistic level: one is the impact of high-temperature thermal annealing on nano-carbons, and another one is the influence of thermal annealing on the change of bonding force between fiber surface and nano-carbons. It should be pointed out that all presented simulations were performed with the help of a large-scale atomic/molecular massively parallel simulator (LAMMPS) module in MedeA simulation software [40]. In this work, models depicting GO, GQDs, and SFs were built based on previous reports [31,41] and shown in Fig. S1(a, b, c), which were used to construct our simulation systems. For all systems, some chains were randomly placed in a simulation box with densities of 1.6 g/cm3, which were shown in Fig. S1(d, e, f, g, h, i). All these systems were equilibrated for 60 ps at a temperature of 300 K using NVT ensemble. Then all samples were heated up to 2800 K with a rate of 10 K/ps to simulate the thermal annealing process. Finally, the systems were kept at 2800 K for 900 ps [28]. The time step was chosen to be 0.2 fs, and the bond order cutoff for molecule identification was 0.3. A Berendsen temperature thermostat with a damping constant of 100 fs was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of nano-carbons on surface defects of SFs
ce:section.ce:para: The process of electrostatic spraying was shown in Fig. 2(a). The spraying solution was broken into small droplets by high-voltage electricity applied to the nozzle. In order to study the movement of charged droplets in electric field, a finite element simulation was used to evaluate the distribution of electric fields. In Fig. 2(b), the electric potential (surface color) and electric field vector (arrows) were revealed. Due to the positive charge applied to the spinning nozzle, the direction for movement of the charged droplet remained consistent with the direction of electric field. According to the arrow of the electric field, charged droplets could be coated on the front and back of the grounded receiving plate. In Fig. 2(a), it was seen that SFs were placed in front of the receiving plate, and during the spraying process, charged droplets moved to the receiving plate, causing some nano-carbons to be coated on the surface of the fibers. However, the nano-carbons could evenly cover the fiber surface due to the uniformity of electrostatic spraying, while only GO or GQDs that repaired defects could enhance the mechanical properties of CFs, which could cause a waste of materials. To improve the utilization rate of particles as fiber-reinforced materials, air with a certain speed was added to the spinning nozzle to make spraying more concentrated. With the assistance of air, the spraying solution was uniformly atomized and formed a Taylor cone, shown in Fig. 2(a). Another finite element simulation was applied to investigate the flow of air between SFs. The direction (arrows) and intensity (surface color) of airflow were shown in Fig. 2(c). It was seen that the airflow could enter the interior of defects on the surface of SFs. It was interesting that the airflow rate in the defects was less than that on the perfect area on fiber surface, which would be beneficial for deposition of GO or GQDs. It should be pointed out that when two SFs were close, the airflow velocity at perfect area on fiber surface increased sharply and even exceeded 10 m/s, which was very unfavorable for particle deposition. Therefore, during the electrostatic spraying process, with the addition of airflow, nano-carbons were more likely to be deposited at defects on surface of SFs, which was beneficial for improving defects on the fiber surface and increasing the mechanical properties of CFs. Due to the different sizes of nano-carbons, the GQDs would enter the inside of the defects, while GO covered the defects and the diagram was shown in the right of Fig. 2(a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology of SFs and CFs with nano-carbons
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of GQDs and GO were observed by TEM and shown in Fig. 3 (a, b). It was seen that GQDs was the sphere with about 2 nm in size and GO was the sheet with a thin and flat wrinkled structure of several microns in size [31,25]. The SEM was adopted to determine the surface morphology of fibers with nano-carbons and shown in Fig. 3(c, d, e, f, g, h, i), and the diameters of fibers were shown in Fig. S2(a). After spraying, there was an obvious presence of nano-carbons on the surface of the fibers, and the fiber diameter had an increase. The pristine SFs possessed a clean surface with a number of defects. After spraying, a wrinkled film was exhibited on the surface of SFs-GO, which covered the defects on fiber surface. As for SFs-GQDs, based on the small size, a large number of GQDs were filled into the defects on the fiber surface and even generated agglomeration, which was consistent with the result of finite element simulation (in Fig. 2(c)). After thermal annealing, the surface of pristine CFs was also clean with defects, which was inherited from SFs [42]. A wrinkled film was found on the surface of CFs-GO, which indicated that GO on the fiber surface could maintain a certain degree of integrity during thermal annealing. For CFs-GQDs, a number of GQDs were found in the surface defects and it was marked by a circle in Fig. 3(i). The morphology of GQDs after thermal annealing was shown in the upper right corner of Fig. 3(i) and the GQDs could continue to maintain spherical shape. It was interesting that the GQDs on the surface of CFs-GQDs shown in Fig. 3(h) looked smaller and less than that on the surface of SFs-GQDs. The reason may be that during the thermal annealing, a large number of atoms (except for the carbon atom) were removed, which was discussed by atomistic ReaxFF simulations later. In order to further characterize the surface of CFs, the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of CFs were obtained and shown in Fig. S2(b). The pores size and specific surface area of CFs were calculated and displayed in Fig. 3(j, k) and Table S2. It could be seen that the distributions of pore diameter for pristine CFs, CFs-GO, and CFs-GQDs were similar. However, compared with pristine CFs, the specific surface area and pore volume (different diameter or overall) of CFs-GO and CFs-GQDs both decreased significantly. It indicated that the nano-carbons could effectively repair surface defects of CFs after thermal annealing, which was consistent with the results of SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface chemical changes on fibers with nano-carbons induced by thermal annealing
ce:section.ce:para: The XPS spectra were used to investigate the changes in functional groups on fiber surface. The C1s XPS spectra were fitted by a Gaussian-Lorentzian peak shape to provide rich information about the functional groups in Fig. 4 and the specific contents of functional groups were displayed in Table S3. In Fig. 4(a, b, c), it was shown that the composition of functional groups on SFs surface had significant changes due to the addition of nano-carbons. The oxygen contents of GO, GQDs, and SFs surface were 30.95%, 22.77%, and 32.5%, respectively. As the nano-carbons (GO or GQDs) were applied on the surface of SFs, the result of C1s XPS spectra indicated a decrease in oxygen-containing functional groups, especially for GQDs. After thermal annealing, the C1s XPS spectra of CFs were shown in Fig. 4(d, e, f). High-temperature heating could remove impurity atoms by releasing gas, which caused the reduction of oxygen-containing functional groups and an increase of CC bonds (including SP2 and SP3). During thermal annealing, a series of reactions occurred through dehydrogenation, denitrification, and deoxygenation reactions to form a graphene structure [41,43]. Based on the XPS result of fibers with nano-carbons, except for improvement of the graphite structure for nano-carbons, it was boldly speculated that complex chemical reactions occurred between the nano-carbons and the CFs to form covalent bonds. The presence of covalent bonds shown in Fig. 4(i) is expected to significantly increase the bonding strength between nano-carbons and CFs. The result of XRD were shown in Fig. 4(g) and Table S4. It could be seen that, compared with pristine CFs, the d002 and LC of CFs-GO and CFs-GQDs both increased. The values of full width at half maximum (FWHM) of CFs-GO and CFs-GQDs were smaller than that of pristine CFs. The FWHM of peak for 002 crystal plane is related to the crystal structure, and a smaller FWHM meant that the peak was sharper, indicating a better crystal structure.

The Raman spectra of CFs were shown in Fig. 4(h) and fitted by using typical carbon bands containing disorder (D, ∼1350 cm-1) and graphitic (G, ∼1580 cm-1) peaks [44,45], which were shown in Fig. S3. D peak reflects the structural defects and disorder of graphitic layers, and G peak is relevant to in-plane stretching of sp2 hybridized carbon pairs [46]. The ID/IG of CFs-GQDs was smaller than that of pristine CFs, which indicated the degree of graphitization for GQDs was higher than that of the CFs surface. However, the ID/IG of CFs-GO was higher than that of pristine CFs, and the reason was the elimination of a large amount of oxygen in GO during the thermal annealing, which took away the carbon atoms and caused defects. The positions and widths of D and G peaks had been calculated and shown in Table S5. Compared with pristine CFs, the positions of D peaks in CFs-GO and CFs-GQDs both were blue shifted with a decrease in FWHM. At the same time, compared with those in pristine CFs, the positions of G peak in CFs-GO and CFs-GQDs were blue shifted and red shifted respectively. The FWHM of G peaks in CFs-GO and CFs-GQDs both had been larger than that in pristine CFs. The in-plane defects and small lateral dimension (La) were both known to broaden the G band [6,47]. The larger FWHM of CFs-GO was attributed to more defects in GO after thermal annealing. As for CFs-GQDs, the larger FWHM may be attributed to changes of La, which needed further verification in next work.

Encouraged by these experimental results, atomistic ReaxFF simulations were performed to investigate the thermal annealing of SFs with nano-carbons for further exploring the possible formation mechanism of covalent bonds between CFs and nano-carbons, and the result was shown in Fig. 5 . First, the structure evolution of GO and GQDs during thermal annealing were shown in Fig. 5(a, b, c, d, e, f). According to the previous report, models for GO and GQDs rich in functional groups had been established in Fig. S1(b, c) [31], and then two GO chains or three GQDs chains were picked out and randomly combined for simulation shown in Fig. S1(d, e). The final structures were shown in Fig. 5(a, d) and it was found that a large amount of gas (such as H2O, H2, N2, CO and CO2) was released. The local amplification of the simulation result for GO after thermal annealing was shown in Fig. 5(b, c). The reduced GO at high temperature could still maintain the network structure formed by rings. The atoms in two GO were represented by different colors. After thermal annealing, the two GO sheets would be integrated by crosslinking structure (marked by a circle in Fig. 5(b, c)), which was formed through dehydrogenation and denitrification reactions with the release of H2O, H2, and N2 [43,48]. The same conclusion was obtained in the simulation of GQDs after heating. Most ring structures of GQDs remained stable with a little of atoms from different GQDs penetrating each other. During thermal annealing, the molecular chains of GQDs could react with each other to form crosslinking, making GQDs a whole (in Fig. 5(e, f)). It should be pointed out that the main released gas was water shown in Fig. S4(a, b), which indicated the oxygen-containing functional groups were responsible for the form of crosslinking between GOs or GQDs.

The simulation models for SFs-GO and SFs-GQDs both contained 32 chains of SFs with one GO chain or two GQDs chains (Fig. S1(f, g, h, i)), respectively. The structures of CFs-GO and CFs-GQDs obtained from simulation were shown in Fig. 5(g, i). The gas generation and a part of CFs structure from simulation were shown in Fig. S4(c, d) and Fig. S5, and the results commendably revealed the formation of graphite-like structures and the overflow of impurity gases, which indicated the accuracy of the simulation. The dangling bonds and oxygen-containing functional groups at the edge of GO induced the formation of bonds between GO edges and the surface of CFs during thermal annealing (in Fig. 5(h)), which was favorable to generating larger graphite structures [28]. And the GO nanosheets were found to exhibit favorable edge chemistry with the formation of rings at the graphene edges. It should be pointed out that after thermal annealing, GO and GQDs were reduced to graphene through deoxidation shown in Fig. S6(a, b, c, d). During thermal annealing, the graphene structure with a large size existed in the final structure of CFs-GO shown in Fig. S6(e). The partial snapshots of the final simulation structure for CFs-GQDs were displayed in Fig. 5(j, k), and atoms in different GQDs were represented by different colors. During thermal annealing, the GQDs could form covalent bonds with CFs, which could increase adhesion between the nano-carbons and fiber surface. In the thermal annealing simulation for SFs-GQDs, the graphene structure with a large size also existed in the final structure of CFs-GQDs shown in Fig. S6(f, g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in bonding force between nano-carbons and fiber surface
ce:section.ce:para: The bonding force between nano-carbons and fibers is an important factor affecting the improvement of mechanical properties. The previous simulation part suggested the forming of covalent bond between nano-carbons and CFs. To test the firmness of the nano-carbons on fiber surface, nano-scratch (as a method for testing the adhesion between two phases) was used to accurately measure the bonding force between nano-carbons and surface of CFs [49]. The GO on the surface of SFs and CFs was observed and the position for scratch was selected, which was shown by the optical microscope in Fig. 6 (a, g). Folded graphite oxide on the fiber surface was found by SEM in Fig. 6(b, h), which was similar to the structure observed by optical microscope. The three-dimensional morphology of the fiber surface with GO (corresponding to the position in the optical microscope) before and after scratch was shown in Fig. 6(c, d, i, j), and the height of testing location along green line was shown in Fig. S7(a, b). On the surface of SFs-GO, the GO was removed completely after scratching with a depth of 20 nm. The lateral displacement and force were shown in Fig. 6(e, f). Based on the horizontal displacement and fiber surface height, the probe started touching GO at the position marked with a rectangle in Fig. 6(f). As the probe moved laterally, the lateral force would gradually change. When the GO was separated from the fiber surface, the lateral force will jump, which was marked with a circle. The result indicated that the bonding force between GO and SFs surface was 3.718 μN. The identical scratch with an inserted depth of 20 nm was carried out on the surface of CFs-GO, but the reduced GO was still on the fiber surface without difference. For separating the reduced GO from the surface of the CFs, indentation depth was increased to improve the force exerted by scratch probe on the GO. Then the inserted depth of the scratch increased to 100 nm, and the surface of fibers after the scratch was shown in Fig. S7(c). The GO on fiber surface emerged with slight structural changes (more compact) compared with GO without a scratch. However, the GO was still fixed on the CFs surface. As the inserted depth increased to 200 nm, there was no significant change in GO on the CFs surface (in Fig. S7(d)), which indicated the forming of a strong bonding force between GO and CFs during thermal annealing. In order to measure the bonding force, scratch was conducted through force control mode with 1000 μN and the lateral displacement and force were shown in Fig. 6(k, l). The mutation of lateral force was marked by a circle and the bonding force was about 154.711 μN. Due to the high bonding force, there would be a significant sudden change in lateral displacement during the separation of GO and fiber (marked by a circle in Fig. 6(k)). The final morphology image of CFs surface after nano-scratch was displayed in Fig. 6(j), and it was seen that there was still a little of GO residue fixed to the CFs surface. As GO was removed, it seemed that part of the CFs surface was also taken away by observing a concavity marked by a circle, which was due to the strong bonding force between GO and CFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement mechanisms of nano-carbons in mechanical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The tensile strength and Young´s modulus of fibers were shown in Fig. 7 (a, b) and Table S6. Compared with pristine SFs, the tensile strength of SFs-GO had no significant change, while the tensile strength of SFs-GQDs had an increase (10.9%). The reason may be that GO covered the defects and there was only intermolecular force between SFs and GO, which was too little to improve tensile strength, while for SFs-GQDs, GQDs entered into defects and accumulated, which could form mechanical interlocking to increase mechanical properties. The Young´s modulus of SFs-GO and SFs-GQDs both had an obvious improvement than pristine SFs, and it indicated that Young´s modulus was sensitive to nano-carbons at defects on fiber surface. After thermal annealing, the tensile strength of CFs-GO and CFs-GQDs enhanced significantly compared with pristine CFs and increased by 24.36% and 15.13%, respectively. The Young´s modulus of CFs-GO and CFs-GQDs also had a significant growth than that of pristine CFs by raising 30.34% and 38.63%, respectively. Compared with SFs, it was found that the improvement of mechanical properties by nano-carbons at defects on the fiber surface was significantly enhanced after thermal annealing. It was suggested that oxygen-containing groups in nano-carbons and SFs could react with each other to form strong covalent bonds between CFs and nano-carbons, which could suppress the diffusion of cracks in CFs during stretching [25,27]. It had been found the fracture of CFs started from the skin region and then transferred inward to the core [50]. During the tensile fracture process, defects on the fiber surface could cause stress concentration and lead to the generation of cracks [9]. Before thermal annealing, due to the weak bonding between the nano-carbons and SFs, the stress concentrated in the defects during the stretching process cannot be effectively transmitted to the nano-carbons, resulting in low mechanical improvement in SFs-GO and SFs-GQDs (compared with pristine SFs). After thermal annealing, the formation of numbers of covalent bonds between CFs and nano-carbons mentioned above had been suggested, and the related schematic diagram was shown in Fig. 7(c). And the bonding force between nano-carbons and CFs became very strong. During the loading process, the strong bonding force could serve as a bridge for stress transmission from CFs to nano-carbons, which could effectively share the stress at the defect to significantly enhance the mechanical properties of CFs-GO and CFs-GQDs (compared with pristine CFs). In addition, covalent cross-linking could also be formed between nano-carbons, making them a sturdy whole (shown in atomistic ReaxFF simulations), which could improve the strength and prevent cracking of reinforcement formed by nano-carbons. It should be pointed out that these two nano-carbons had different ways of enhancing mechanical properties of fibers: GO was regarded as a bridge to connect both ends of the defects, while GQDs were used to fill the defects. The tensile strength of CFs-GO was higher than that of CFs-GQDs, indicating that the bridging mode of GO may be more advantageous for improving tensile strength than the filling mode. However, the Young´s modulus of CFs-GO was less than that of CFs-GQDs. The reason was that GO on the surface of CFs possessed a wrinkled structure, and during the tensile fracture process, the GO sheet was straightened, which could cause greater deformation of CFs to weaken the Young's modulus.

In order to further investigate the enhancement mechanism of nano-carbons, the 2D finite element simulation by ABAQUS was applied to discuss the stress distribution of CFs during the stretching process. It should be pointed out that GQDs could generate cross-linking with each other to form a whole during thermal annealing, therefore the GQDs in defects were set as a whole structure in the simulation. The result of finite element simulation about mechanical properties was shown in Fig. 8 . In Fig. 8(b, c), it could be seen that the stress was mainly concentrated at the defect, especially at both ends of the defect, and the stress gradually decreased as it moved away from the surface of CFs. In Fig. 8(b, e, h), due to the repair of fiber surface defects by nano-carbons, the stress diffusion from defects to the interior of CFs was weakened, especially in CFs-GO. In Fig. 8(c, f, i), it was found that with the introduction of nano-carbons, the stress at defect on the fiber surface significantly decreased and the maximum stresses on whole models for pristine CFs, CFs-GQDs, and CFs-GO were 2.45×10–5 MPa, 2.03×10–5 MPa and 0.79×10–5 MPa respectively. In a word, the addition of nano-carbons at the defect on fiber surface could reduce stress and weaken stress diffusion into the interior of fiber, which could explain the increase in mechanical properties of CFs-GQDs and CFs-GO than the pristine CFs.

In addition to the formation of suggested covalent bonds, the enhancement of CFs by nano-carbons may be related to the size of particles, which could determine the enhancement mechanism: bridging or filling. The different enhancement mechanisms for simulation were demonstrated in Fig. 8(b, e, h). It was interesting that GO was more effective in relieving stress at fiber defects than GQDs, which indicated that bridging may be more beneficial for improving the mechanical properties of CFs than filling. And it was consistent with the results of tensile strength of CFs. To better understand the impact of particles with different sizes on mechanical properties, the diffusion process of cracks was plotted in Fig. 9 . On the surface of pristine CFs, cracks were generated at the defect location and spread severely towards the interior of the fibers, which lead to fracture and failure of CFs. On the surface of CFs-GQDs, the stress concentration at the original defect was effectively alleviated, resulting in smaller cracks and weaker internal diffusion. On the surface of CFs-GO, cracks were further reduced and concentrated at both ends of the original defect, and it could be seen that the propagation of cracks towards the core was further weakened.

In order to repair surface defects on CFs, the GO and GQDs which have similar structures but different sizes were selected to be coated on SFs through electrostatic spraying and then thermally annealed. With the assistance of airflow during electrostatic spraying, the spraying could be more concentrated and particles could be better coated at defects, which significantly reduced the specific surface area and pore volume. The tensile strength of CFs with GO and GQDs increased by 24.36% and 15.13% with Young´s modulus increasing by 30.34% and 38.63%, respectively. After thermal annealing, the formation of covalent bonds between the CFs and nano-carbons (GO or GQDs) was suggested by XPS and atomistic ReaxFF MD simulation. The molecular chains in various nano-carbons could form crosslinking with each other to make it a whole, and crosslinking was also formed between nano-carbons and the surface of CFs to improve the firmness of nano-carbons on CFs surface, which could be responsible for the enhancement in mechanical properties. The bonding force between GO and fibers was measured by nano-scratch, and it was about 154.711 μN in CFs-GO, which had a huge increase than that of SFs-GO about 3.718 μN. To further explain the reinforcement mechanism of nano-carbons, a finite element simulation was applied to research stress distribution on CFs. The addition of GO or GQDs could reduce the value and diffusion of stress, and the maximum stress on defects for pristine CFs, CFs-GQDs, and CFs-GO was 2.45×10–5 MPa, 2.03×10–5 MPa, and 0.79×10–5 MPa. The bridging mode of GO was more advantageous for reducing the stress than filling mode of GQDs. Directional introducing of coatings on the surface defect of SFs can significantly enhance the firmness of coating on the surface of resultant CFs and possesses an enormous potential for manufacturing high-performance CFs.

Tianshuai Ma: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Ruiqi Shao: Project administration, Resources. Amna Siddique: Investigation, Resources, Visualization. Wei Wang: Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Tianyu Li: Resources, Software. Wanwei Jiang: Resources, Supervision. Xiaoyuan Pei: Resources, Supervision. Zhiwei Xu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12205218 and 12102300) and Nantong Science and Technology Project (JC12022089). We would like to thank the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University for X-ray Diffractometer, Thermal Field-Emission Scanning Electron Microscope, Transmission Electron Microscope and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.104783.
